title: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Rise in Drug-Resistant Fungal Infections
items.content: The increase in fungal infections is attributed to factors such as the AIDS pandemic, widespread use of antibacterial agents, and immunosuppressive treatments. Notably, there has been a rise in drug-resistant species like azole- and/or echinocandin-resistant Candida glabrata, Candida auris, and azole-resistant Aspergillus fumigatus.
keyPoints: Increase in fungal infections due to AIDS pandemic, antibacterial agents, and immunosuppressive treatments
Rise in drug-resistant species: azole- and/or echinocandin-resistant Candida glabrata, Candida auris, azole-resistant Aspergillus fumigatus
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.title: Classification and Acquisition of Fungal Infections
items.content: Fungal infections are classified based on anatomic location (mucocutaneous vs. deep organ) and epidemiology (endemic vs. opportunistic). Mucocutaneous infections cause morbidity but are rarely fatal, while deep organ infections have high mortality rates. Opportunistic mycoses occur in immunosuppressed hosts due to fungi like Candida and Aspergillus, whereas endemic fungi typically affect immunocompromised patients.
keyPoints: Classification: mucocutaneous vs. deep organ; endemic vs. opportunistic
Mucocutaneous infections cause morbidity but are rarely fatal
Deep organ infections have high mortality rates
Opportunistic mycoses occur in immunosuppressed hosts (Candida, Aspergillus)
Endemic fungi affect immunocompromised patients
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.title: Role of CLR/SYK/CARD9 Pathway in Fungal Recognition and Defense
items.content: The CLR/SYK/CARD9 signaling pathway is crucial for recognizing fungal polysaccharides, leading to proinflammatory responses, leukocyte recruitment, inflammasome activation, and Th17 cell differentiation. CARD9 deficiency results in severe mucocutaneous and invasive fungal diseases without predisposition to other infections.
keyPoints: CLR/SYK/CARD9 pathway mediates fungal polysaccharide recognition.
CARD9-deficient patients develop specific fungal infections like chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis, CNS infections by Candida, Aspergillus, phaeohyphomycetes, and deep dermatophytosis.
Clinical use of SYK inhibitors may cause opportunistic fungal disease.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Importance of IL-17 in Mucosal Fungal Defense
items.content: IL-17-producing lymphoid cells are essential for mucosal defense against Candida by promoting epithelial cell production of antimicrobial peptides. Deficiency or inhibition of IL-17 signaling leads to mucosal candidiasis, particularly in AIDS patients and those with specific genetic mutations.
keyPoints: IL-17 is critical for protection at the mucosal interface against Candida.
Deficiencies in IL-17 signaling (e.g., mutations in IL17F, IL17RA) cause mucosal candidiasis.
Conditions like APECED with autoantibodies to IL-17A and IL-22 also predispose to mucosal candidiasis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Neutrophils' Role in Controlling Invasive Fungal Infections
items.content: Neutrophils are vital for controlling invasive infections by Aspergillus and Candida. Neutropenia, such as that induced by chemotherapy or seen in hematopoietic stem cell transplantation recipients, increases the risk of these infections.
keyPoints: Neutrophils are critical for controlling invasive fungal infections like those caused by Aspergillus and Candida.
Chemotherapy-induced neutropenia and allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation increase the risk of invasive aspergillosis and candidiasis.
Chronic granulomatous disease, due to NADPH oxidase complex mutations, carries a ~40% lifetime risk for invasive aspergillosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Immune Modulation on Susceptibility to Fungal Infections
items.content: Patients with AIDS, those receiving glucocorticoids, or individuals with specific genetic mutations affecting the IL-12/IFN-$ \gamma $ signaling axis are at increased risk for severe fungal infections. This is due to impaired macrophage function and other immune responses critical for controlling intramacrophagic fungi.
keyPoints: AIDS patients and glucocorticoid users have compromised lymphocytes and macrophages, increasing infection risk.
Mutations in IL12RB1, IFNGR1, IFNGR2, STAT1, IRF8, or GATA2 lead to susceptibility due to impaired IFN-$ \gamma $ signaling.
Emapalumab, JAK inhibitors, autoantibodies to IFN-$ \gamma $, and TNF-$ \alpha $ targeting biologics increase infection risk by affecting macrophage activation.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Modalities for Fungal Infections
items.content: Definitive diagnosis of fungal infections requires histopathologic identification and culture. Various stains like PAS, GMS, and Gram stain are used to identify fungi in tissues. Positive India ink staining is diagnostic for cryptococcosis.
keyPoints: Histopathologic identification and parallel culture are required for definitive diagnosis.
PAS and GMS stains are commonly used; Candida can be seen on Gram-stained smears.
India ink stain of CSF is diagnostic for cryptococcosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Considerations in Fungal Culture and Antigen Testing
items.content: A positive fungal culture may indicate colonization or contamination, not necessarily infection. The lysis-centrifugation technique enhances sensitivity for detecting fungi like Candida and Histoplasma in blood cultures. MALDI-TOF-MS is widely used for fungal speciation from cultures.
keyPoints: Positive fungal cultures can signify colonization or contamination; context matters.
Lysis-centrifugation increases sensitivity for detecting Candida and other fungi in blood.
MALDI-TOF-MS is extensively used for detection and speciation of cultured fungi.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Why is early initiation of antifungal therapy critical?
items.content: Early initiation of appropriate antifungal therapy significantly influences the outcome in fungal infections such as candidemia, aspergillosis, and mucormycosis. This approach, combined with source control measures like catheter removal or surgical debridement, is vital for improving prognosis.
keyPoints: Early initiation of antifungal therapy is crucial for favorable outcomes in candidemia, aspergillosis, and mucormycosis.
Source control methods include removing central venous catheters in candidemia and surgical debridement in mucormycosis.
Achieving immune reconstitution (e.g., neutrophil recovery or antiretroviral therapy) is essential for a favorable prognosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.title: How does amphotericin B work and what are its limitations?
items.content: Amphotericin B (AmB) is a broad-spectrum antifungal agent that works by forming extramembranous aggregates to extract ergosterol from fungal cell membranes, leading to cell death. Despite its efficacy, AmB has significant toxicity issues, particularly nephrotoxicity and infusion-related reactions like fever and chills. Lipid formulations of AmB have reduced these toxicities but are cost-prohibitive in some regions.
keyPoints: Amphotericin B forms extramembranous aggregates to extract ergosterol from fungal membranes, causing cell death.
It is the preferred agent for mucormycosis, fusariosis, and induction therapy for cryptococcal meningitis.
Toxicities include renal damage and infusion-related reactions (fever, chills, thrombosis).
Lipid formulations like liposomal AmB reduce toxicity but are costly, limiting their use in developing countries.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.title: Mechanism and Fungistatic Nature of Azoles
items.content: Azoles inhibit lanosterol 14α-demethylase, disrupting ergosterol synthesis in fungal cell membranes. This leads to toxic sterol accumulation and growth arrest, making azoles fungistatic rather than fungicidal.
keyPoints: Inhibition of lanosterol 14α-demethylase
Disruption of ergosterol synthesis
Accumulation of toxic sterol intermediates
Fungistatic action
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.title: Fluconazole Use and Limitations
items.content: Fluconazole is preferred for coccidioidal meningitis, mucosal candidiasis, and prophylaxis in high-risk liver and bone marrow transplant recipients. However, it lacks activity against molds and some Candida strains, with recent concerns about its use during pregnancy.
keyPoints: Preferred for coccidioidal meningitis
Used for mucosal candidiasis and prophylaxis
No activity against molds or C. glabrata/C. krusei
Linked to miscarriage and stillbirth in pregnant women
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.title: Voriconazole's Broad Spectrum and Monitoring Needs
items.content: Voriconazole is effective against a wide range of fungi, including Aspergillus and Scedosporium. It requires careful monitoring due to its metabolism by CYP enzymes and potential for significant interpatient variability in drug levels.
keyPoints: Broad antifungal activity (C. glabrata, C. krusei, Aspergillus)
Penetrates most body fluids
Metabolized by CYP2C9, CYP3A4, and CYP2C19
Requires monitoring due to genetic variability in metabolism
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which factor has NOT contributed to the increase in human fungal infections worldwide?
items.explanation: The increase is due to the AIDS pandemic, widespread use of antibacterial agents, and introduction of cytotoxic agents.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.question: Which fungal species is known to form only yeasts in tissue?
items.explanation: Candida glabrata and C. auris form only yeasts in tissue, unlike other Candida species.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.question: How are fungal infections classified based on anatomic location?
items.explanation: Fungal infections are classified as mucocutaneous and deep organ based on anatomic location.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which signaling pathway mediates fungal polysaccharide recognition and orchestrates proinflammatory mediator production, leukocyte recruitment, inflammasome activation, and Th17 cell differentiation upon fungal invasion?
items.explanation: The CLR/SYK/CARD9 signaling pathway is responsible for these functions.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: What type of infections does CARD9 deficiency specifically predispose individuals to without a predisposition to other infections, autoimmunity, allergy, or cancer?
items.explanation: CARD9 deficiency causes severe mucocutaneous and invasive fungal disease.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Which cytokine-producing cells are crucial for protection against mucosal Candida invasion by driving epithelial cell production of antimicrobial peptides?
items.explanation: IL-17-producing lymphoid cells are critical for mucosal protection against Candida.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: In patients with chronic granulomatous disease, what is the lifetime risk of developing invasive aspergillosis?
items.explanation: Chronic granulomatous disease carries a ~40% lifetime risk for invasive aspergillosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following factors predispose to infection with intramacrophagic fungi?
options: Inherited impairment of the IL-12/IFN-$ \gamma $ signaling axis.
Use of antibiotics targeting Gram-positive bacteria.
High levels of serum immunoglobulin E (IgE).
items.explanation: Inherited impairment of the IL-12/IFN-$ \gamma $ signaling axis predisposes to infection with intramacrophagic fungi.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which stain is most reliable for identifying Candida in tissue smears?
items.explanation: Candida is visible on Gram-stained tissue smears, unlike other fungi.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What diagnostic test is FDA-approved for detecting Candida in blood?
items.explanation: T2 magnetic resonance is FDA-approved for detection of Candida in blood.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which test has a high negative predictive value for invasive candidiasis but lacks specificity?
items.explanation: The $ \beta $-glucan test has a high negative predictive value for invasive candidiasis but lacks specificity.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of action of amphotericin B (AmB) in treating fungal infections?
options: Forms extramembranous sponge-like aggregates that extract fungal ergosterol from lipid bilayers
Blocks protein synthesis in fungal cells
items.explanation: AmB forms extramembranous sponge-like aggregates that extract fungal ergosterol, disrupting the cell membrane.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.question: Which of the following infections is amphotericin B (AmB) considered the preferred antifungal agent for induction therapy?
items.explanation: AmB is the preferred antifungal agent for induction therapy in cryptococcal meningitis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.question: What are the primary toxicities associated with intravenous preparations of amphotericin B (AmB)?
options: Renal toxicity and infusion-related reactions like fever and chills
items.explanation: AmB intravenous preparations are primarily associated with renal toxicity and infusion-related reactions such as fever and chills.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.question: Which azole is preferred for the treatment of coccidioidal meningitis?
items.explanation: Fluconazole is preferred for the treatment of coccidioidal meningitis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.question: Which azole has been linked to miscarriage and stillbirth when administered in low doses during pregnancy?
items.explanation: Low doses of fluconazole have been linked to miscarriage and stillbirth in pregnant women.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.question: Which azole has a new formulation called SUBAitraconazole?
items.explanation: SUBAitraconazole is the improved formulation of itraconazole.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.question: Which azole is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor and can lead to significant drug interactions?
items.explanation: Itraconazole is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor, leading to significant drug interactions.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Opportunistic mycoses are caused by fungi that are not part of the normal human microbiota.
items.explanation: Opportunistic mycoses are often caused by fungi like Candida and Aspergillus, which can be part of the human microbiota.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY
items.statement: Endemic fungi typically cause severe illness in immunocompetent hosts.
items.explanation: Endemic fungi usually cause self-limited disease in immunocompetent hosts but can cause severe illness in immunosuppressed patients.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · Section 16 Fungal Infections | Pathogenesis, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Fungal Infections | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Inherited deficiency of Toll-like receptor signaling leads to spontaneous fungal disease.
items.explanation: Human inherited deficiency of TLR signaling does not lead to spontaneous fungal disease.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: Vaginal candidiasis can develop in the setting of antibiotic treatment.
items.explanation: Vaginal candidiasis develops with antibiotic treatment, unlike oropharyngeal and esophageal candidiasis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.statement: A positive India ink stain of cerebrospinal fluid is diagnostic for histoplasmosis.
items.explanation: A positive India ink stain of CSF is diagnostic for cryptococcosis, not histoplasmosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The galactomannan test is useful for diagnosing aspergillosis.
items.explanation: The galactomannan test, especially in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, is useful for the diagnosis of aspergillosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Lipid formulations of amphotericin B have largely replaced the original deoxycholate formulation in resource-rich settings.
items.explanation: The introduction of lipid AmB formulations has reduced toxicities and they are preferred over the deoxycholate formulation in resource-rich settings.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.statement: Amphotericin B is available in a licensed oral formulation.
items.explanation: There is no licensed oral formulation of amphotericin B.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.statement: Fluconazole is effective against most endemic dimorphic fungi.
items.explanation: Fluconazole has no activity against molds and most endemic dimorphic fungi.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.statement: Voriconazole is the preferred agent for the treatment of aspergillosis.
items.explanation: Voriconazole is indeed the preferred agent for treating aspergillosis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.assertion: Neutrophils are critical for control of invasive infections caused by Aspergillus.
items.reason: Patients with chemotherapy-induced neutropenia are at risk for invasive aspergillosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why neutrophils are critical.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: In APECED, remission of candidiasis can be achieved with JAK inhibition.
items.reason: APECED features autoantibodies to IL-17A and IL-22.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for remission of candidiasis.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.assertion: The IFN-$ \gamma $-targeting monoclonal antibody emapalumab predisposes to infection with intramacrophagic fungi.
items.reason: Emapalumab blocks IFN-$ \gamma $-dependent cellular responses, which are crucial for macrophage activation against these pathogens.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Hematoxylin and eosin stains can reliably identify fungi in tissue.
items.reason: These stains define accompanying histologic features of fungal disease but are insufficient for reliable identification.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because hematoxylin and eosin stains cannot reliably identify fungi, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · PATHOGENESIS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Amphotericin B remains the broadest-spectrum antifungal agent.
items.reason: Its fungicidal mechanism of action involves forming extramembranous sponge-like aggregates that extract fungal ergosterol from lipid bilayers.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains why AmB has a broad spectrum of activity.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · ANTIFUNGAL DRUGS | AMPHOTERICIN B

items.assertion: Posaconazole has broader antifungal activity than voriconazole.
items.reason: Posaconazole includes activity against Mucorales, which voriconazole does not cover.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES

items.assertion: Fluconazole is used for treating candidemia in critically ill patients.
items.reason: Fluconazole is as efficacious as AmB in non-critically ill or immunosuppressed patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; fluconazole is not recommended for critically ill patients.
items.reference: Pathogenesis Diagnosis and Treatment of Fungal Infections · AZOLES
